Messages list on device '/dev/mapper/36005076812808157b80000000000124b':
0	hdbna1	clear	hdbna1.isicc.de.ibm.com
1	hdbnb1	clear
# pcs cluster start
Starting Cluster...
# pcs status
Cluster name: SAP_CLUSTER
Status of pacemakerd: 'Pacemaker is running' (last updated 2024-08-05 15:34:51 +02:00)
Cluster Summary:
  * Stack: corosync
  * Current DC: hdbnb1 (version 2.1.5-9.el9_2.4-a3f44794f94) - partition with quorum
  * Last updated: Mon Aug  5 15:34:51 2024
  * Last change:  Mon Aug  5 14:59:59 2024 by root via cibadmin on hdbna1
  * 2 nodes configured
  * 1 resource instance configured

Node List:
  * Online: [ hdbna1 hdbnb1 ]

Full List of Resources:
  * res_fence_sbd	(stonith:fence_sbd):	 Started hdbnb1

Daemon Status:
  corosync: active/disabled
  pacemaker: active/disabled
  pcsd: active/enabled
  sbd: active/enabled

 

Configuring the cluster to make an SAP solution highly available

At this point, the setup of the base cluster is complete.
From here, you can continue with the standard documentation to configure the cluster for one of the supported SAP high availability scenarios. The Red Hat HA Solutions for SAP HANA, S/4HANA and NetWeaver based SAP Applications provides references to documentation for implementing the supported scenarios.

A comprehensive set of documentation for implementing high availability for a range of SAP application scenarios is also available at Implementing High Availability for SAP Applications on IBM Power Virtual Server. Although this documentation is written for implementing a high-availability cluster running on IBM Power Virtual Server in the IBM Cloud, the cluster configuration of the SAP application scenarios is the same whether implemented in a cloud or in an on-premises environment. The main difference is the setup of the base cluster.

The following output shows the final cluster status of an SAP HANA System Replication cluster after following the instructions in Configuring SAP HANA Scale-Up System Replication in a RHEL HA Add-On cluster.

 

# pcs status --full
Cluster name: SAP_CLUSTER
Status of pacemakerd: 'Pacemaker is running' (last updated 2024-08-06 11:17:53 +02:00)
Cluster Summary:
  * Stack: corosync
  * Current DC: hdbna1 (1) (version 2.1.5-9.el9_2.4-a3f44794f94) - partition with quorum
  * Last updated: Tue Aug  6 11:17:54 2024
  * Last change:  Tue Aug  6 11:17:34 2024 by root via crm_attribute on hdbna1
  * 2 nodes configured
  * 6 resource instances configured

Node List:
  * Node hdbna1 (1): online, feature set 3.16.2
  * Node hdbnb1 (2): online, feature set 3.16.2

Full List of Resources:
  * res_fence_sbd	(stonith:fence_sbd):	 Started hdbna1
  * Clone Set: SAPHanaTopology_HDB_00-clone [SAPHanaTopology_HDB_00]:
    * SAPHanaTopology_HDB_00	(ocf:heartbeat:SAPHanaTopology):	 Started hdbna1
    * SAPHanaTopology_HDB_00	(ocf:heartbeat:SAPHanaTopology):	 Started hdbnb1
  * Clone Set: SAPHana_HDB_00-clone [SAPHana_HDB_00] (promotable):
    * SAPHana_HDB_00	(ocf:heartbeat:SAPHana):	 Promoted hdbna1
    * SAPHana_HDB_00	(ocf:heartbeat:SAPHana):	 Unpromoted hdbnb1
  * vip_HDB_00	(ocf:heartbeat:IPaddr2):	 Started hdbna1

Node Attributes:
  * Node: hdbna1 (1):
    * hana_hdb_clone_state            	: PROMOTED
    * hana_hdb_op_mode                	: logreplay
    * hana_hdb_remoteHost             	: hdbnb1
    * hana_hdb_roles                  	: 4:P:master1:master:worker:master
    * hana_hdb_site                   	: SiteA
    * hana_hdb_srah                   	: -
    * hana_hdb_srmode                 	: syncmem
    * hana_hdb_sync_state             	: PRIM
    * hana_hdb_version                	: 2.00.079.00
    * hana_hdb_vhost                  	: hdbna1
    * lpa_hdb_lpt                     	: 1722935854
    * master-SAPHana_HDB_00           	: 150
  * Node: hdbnb1 (2):
    * hana_hdb_clone_state            	: DEMOTED
    * hana_hdb_op_mode                	: logreplay
    * hana_hdb_remoteHost             	: hdbna1
    * hana_hdb_roles                  	: 4:S:master1:master:worker:master
    * hana_hdb_site                   	: SiteB
    * hana_hdb_srah                   	: -
    * hana_hdb_srmode                 	: syncmem
    * hana_hdb_sync_state             	: SOK
    * hana_hdb_version                	: 2.00.079.00
    * hana_hdb_vhost                  	: hdbnb1
    * lpa_hdb_lpt                     	: 30
    * master-SAPHana_HDB_00           	: 100

Migration Summary:

Tickets:

PCSD Status:
  hdbna1: Online
  hdbnb1: Online

Daemon Status:
  corosync: active/disabled
  pacemaker: active/disabled
  pcsd: active/enabled
  sbd: active/enabled

 

Summary

This blog post describes a sample implementation of a RHEL HA Add-On cluster on IBM Power10 servers using SBD fencing. SBD fencing simplifies the cluster setup and operation and is the recommended fencing method where available.
The resulting basic cluster implementation, combined with the proven reliability of IBM Power, provides the perfect platform for making a wide range of SAP application scenarios highly available.

entry.title: Strengthening High Availability for SAP in AWS Environments
entry.content:
SAP has over 437,000 customers in 190 countries. It is a leading provider of enterprise applications and supply chain management. SAP systems are integral to business operations and demand a highly reliable infrastructure to ensure uninterrupted service. Implementing high availability (HA) in AWS requires well thought out planning, especially when integrating tools like Pacemaker to manage SAP services and databases. This guide will delve into advanced strategies to enhance the robustness of HA setups, ensuring that SAP systems remain operational and resilient against disruptions.

In this blog with coauthor Santa Uppala (Architect), we will focus on Suse implementation,  most of which can be easily adjusted to Redhat. We will share the lessons learned through years of real customer experiences, combined with the latest AWS and SAP guidelines. 

We start with a way to simplify resources in HA setup called "Simple-Mount". Then we continue on to other important topics.

Simple-Mount Configuration

SUSE has introduced a new ASCS and ERS cluster solution using a simple-mount file system structure. This simplifies the NFS shared file system and relies on SYSTEMD for managing filesystem dependencies, enhancing robustness over traditional configurations. This is now the recommended default for both NetWeaver ENSA1 and S/4HANA ENSA2 (Amazon Web Services) (SUSE Documentation).

Traditionally, we control EFS, for example, /usr/sap/SID/ASCS00 and /usr/sap/SID/ERS10, via cluster, and mount the relevant file system in one node of the cluster where the service is active. We have found that EFS could become a bottleneck and even cause instability issue when doing switching over. We have been trying to find a simplified approach.  Suse recently proposed Simple Mount Architecture where we no longer need to control EFS resources by cluster. Instead, we simply mount EFS on all cluster nodes.

Here is a description of how to manage EFS.

  1. Maintain /etc/fstab on all nodes of the cluster. As a viable alternative, we could use systemd resource control for the same purpose and enable the resource so filesystems are mounted post reboot. 
  2. Mount on all nodes. Make sure to check if all filesystems are automatically mounted after reboot.
  3. In cluster resource groups, do not configure resources to manage EFS. Remove such configurations if already existing.

For more information, please refer to

Simple Mount Structure for SAP Application Platform | SUSE Communities

 

Cluster Failover Monitoring

It is critical to monitor HA and got alerted when it happens so basis could check SAP systems to ensure all SAP systems are operational and all batch jobs are properly handled. Here we show a concrete case of failover monitoring and Service*Now incident generation.

When cluster failover happens, cluster manager will perform a series of actions to trigger the main application service failover from one node to the other node in the cluster. From AWS perspective, there is a ReplaceRoute event, which we could capture and then setup necessary actions to alert administrators.

From AWS console, we create a cloudwatch rule which will be triggered by ReplaceRoute event. And this event will designate a SNS target that can be subscribed by email thread and Service*Now incident trigger.

Here is the setup,

Event pattern

{
  "source": ["aws.ec2"],
  "detail-type": ["AWS API Call via CloudTrail"],
  "detail": {
    "eventSource": ["ec2.amazonaws.com"],
    "eventName": ["ReplaceRoute"]
  }
}

In this event rule, we setup its target to a SNS topic, which Service*Now could subscribe and trigger incidents and alerts.

 

Monitor SAP Resources using Cloudwatch Application Insights

Using Application Insights we can monitor SAP Resources such as Central services and HANA DB operational status. Dashboards can be created as a single pane of glass to monitor the performance and availability at database, resources, OS and AWS Infrastructure layers. Additionally, it correlates and analyzes log data to identify warnings of common problems such as sync state,  resource utilization, replication issues and critical process failures in the HANA database.

Redundant Cluster Communication:

It is recommended to define a redundant communication channel (a second ring) in Corosync for SUSE clusters. This improves fault tolerance by providing a backup communication path between cluster nodes (Amazon AWS Documentation).

e.g.

nodelist {
        node {
                ring0_addr: 11.0.1.132
                ring1_addr: 11.0.1.75
               nodeid: 1
        }

        node {
                ring0_addr: 11.0.2.139
                ring1_addr: 11.0.2.35
                nodeid: 2
        }
}

We can refer to

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sap/latest/sap-hana/sap-hana-on-aws-cluster-configuration.html

The conversion from single ring to dual ring can be done without any downtime. Here are the steps:

  1. Get another set of IPs by adding IP from AWS console
    EC2 -> Networking -> Manage IP addresses -> Assign more IPs.
  2. Change corosync by using the following command on all nodes
    crm corosync edit
  3. Enable the new configuration
    corosync-cfgtool -R
  4. Validate the configuration
    corosync-cmapctl

If outage is allowed, we can do a “crm cluster stop” and make changes to /etc/corosync/corosync.conf, 
then do “crm cluster start”

Monitoring Location Constraints using Custom Scripts

One of the most important aspects in the HA Resource failover is the location constraints. Sometimes, back and forth failover creates a negative location constraint in the CRM config file which acts as a lock mechanism instructing the DC node not to failover the resources at any given circumstances, this will result in the unsuccessful failover if undetected. In order to avoid this we need to setup a custom monitoring to detect these location lock entries and delete it in timely manner. 

Cluster Resource Configuration via AWS Launch Wizard

AWS Launch Wizard has become increasing popular and helped cloud architect/engineers to automate the provisioning the SAP clusters with pacemaker. Here is a reference on the topic.
Simplify, Accelerate, and Automate SAP Deployment Using AWS Launch Wizard for SAP

We illustrate the results from using AWS Launch Wizard for SAP, where all the best practices and latest recommendations are already built in. We will illustrate with ASCS/ERS instances as it embodies the recommendations such as simple mount. 

Here is the crm status,

Node List:
* Online: [ nodeps01 nodeps02 ]

Full List of Resources:
* res_AWS_STONITH (stonith:external/ec2): Started nodeps02
* Resource Group: grp_DWS_ASCS01:
* rsc_ip_DWS_ASCS01 (ocf::suse:aws-vpc-move-ip): Started nodeps02
* rsc_sap_DWS_ASCS01 (ocf::heartbeat:SAPInstance): Started nodeps02
* Resource Group: grp_DWS_ERS10:
* rsc_ip_DWS_ERS10 (ocf::suse:aws-vpc-move-ip): Started nodeps01
* rsc_sap_DWS_ERS10 (ocf::heartbeat:SAPInstance): Started nodeps01


Now the whole configuration as a reference,

node 1: nodeps01 \
attributes maintenance=off
node 2: nodeps02 \
attributes maintenance=off
primitive res_AWS_STONITH stonith:external/ec2 \
op start interval=0 timeout=180 \
op stop interval=0 timeout=180 \
op monitor interval=120 timeout=60 \
params tag=dwspacemaker profile=cluster pcmk_delay_max=15
primitive rsc_ip_DWS_ASCS01 ocf:suse:aws-vpc-move-ip \
params ip=192.168.10.20 routing_table=rtb-12345678 interface=eth0 profile=cluster \
op start interval=0 timeout=180 \
op stop interval=0 timeout=180 \
op monitor interval=60 timeout=60 \
meta target-role=Started
primitive rsc_ip_DWS_ERS10 ocf:suse:aws-vpc-move-ip \
params ip=192.168.10.30 routing_table=rtb-12345678 interface=eth0 profile=cluster \
op start interval=0 timeout=180 \
op stop interval=0 timeout=180 \
op monitor interval=60 timeout=60 \
meta target-role=Started
primitive rsc_sap_DWS_ASCS01 SAPInstance \
operations $id=rsc_sap_DWS_ASCS01-operations \
op monitor interval=120 timeout=60 on-fail=restart \
params InstanceName=DWS_ASCS01_nodepa01 START_PROFILE="/sapmnt/DWS/profile/DWS_ASCS01_nodepa01" AUTOMATIC_RECOVER=false \
meta resource-stickiness=5000 failure-timeout=60 migration-threshold=1 priority=10 target-role=Started
primitive rsc_sap_DWS_ERS10 SAPInstance \
operations $id=rsc_sap_DWS_ERS10-operations \
op monitor interval=120 timeout=60 on-fail=restart \
params InstanceName=DWS_ERS10_nodeer01 START_PROFILE="/sapmnt/DWS/profile/DWS_ERS10_nodeer01" AUTOMATIC_RECOVER=false IS_ERS=true \
meta priority=1000 target-role=Started
group grp_DWS_ASCS01 rsc_ip_DWS_ASCS01 rsc_sap_DWS_ASCS01 \
meta resource-stickiness=3000 target-role=Started
group grp_DWS_ERS10 rsc_ip_DWS_ERS10 rsc_sap_DWS_ERS10
colocation col_sap_DWS_no_both -5000: grp_DWS_ERS10 grp_DWS_ASCS01
location loc_sap_DWS_failover_to_ers rsc_sap_DWS_ASCS01 \
rule 2000: runs_ers_DWS eq 1
order ord_sap_DWS_first_start_ascs Optional: rsc_sap_DWS_ASCS01:start rsc_sap_DWS_ERS10:stop symmetrical=false
property cib-bootstrap-options: \
have-watchdog=false \
dc-version="2.1.5+20221208.a3f44794f-150500.6.14.4-2.1.5+20221208.a3f44794f" \
cluster-infrastructure=corosync \
maintenance-mode=false \
stonith-enabled=true \
stonith-action=off \
stonith-timeout=600s \
last-lrm-refresh=1716118611
rsc_defaults rsc-options: \
resource-stickiness=1 \
migration-threshold=3
op_defaults op-options: \
timeout=600 \
record-pending=trueINFO: "config" is accepted as "configure"

Conclusion:

Maintaining healthy cluster configuration is paramount to the stability of SAP systems. We have observed positive results after addressing the points outlined in this blog. It is an iterative process, and we will continue this innovative journey to minimize system interruptions, ensure proper alerts, and enhance our ability to handle exceptions.

References:

Simple Mount Structure for SAP Application Platform

First certified simple-mount cluster ever!

SAP S/4 HANA - Enqueue Replication 2 High Availability Cluster With Simple Mount

Updated Guidance for SAP Pacemaker configuration – ABAP SAP Central Services (ASCS)

SAP NetWeaver on AWS High Availability for SLES Planning

entry.title: Choosing the Right Path for Your S/4HANA Transformation: Greenfield, Brownfield, or Bluefield?
entry.content:
The journey to SAP S/4HANA can take different forms, depending on your organization's goals, existing system landscape, and appetite for change. Whether you're looking to redesign your processes entirely, preserve your current system, or take a more flexible, hybrid approach, selecting the right migration strategy is critical.

In this blog, we'll explore the key differences between greenfield, brownfield, and bluefield conversions, helping you determine which path aligns best with your business needs as you transition to S/4HANA.

Greenfield Approach:

A greenfield approach involves starting from scratch. It’s a fresh implementation of S/4HANA, where the system is built without carrying over any existing customizations, processes, or data. Companies use greenfield when they want to redesign their business processes entirely or when they are moving from a non-SAP system to S/4HANA.

Brownfield Approach:

A brownfield approach involves a system conversion, upgrading the existing SAP ERP (ECC) system to S/4HANA. It retains most of the existing customizations, processes, and data, but moves them onto the S/4HANA platform.

Companies opt for brownfield when they want to retain existing processes and customizations but upgrade to S/4HANA.

Features:

  • It's an in-place migration, where you upgrade the existing system rather than rebuilding or starting fresh.
  • Keeps historical data, configurations, and custom developments in place.
  • Suitable for companies looking to maintain continuity and minimize disruptions.
  • Less costly and faster than a Greenfield approach but may carry over technical debt or outdated processes.

Use Case: When an organization wants to preserve its existing investments and customizations while upgrading the core to S/4HANA.

Bluefield Approach:

The bluefield approach is a hybrid of greenfield and brownfield. It involves selective data and process migration, allowing companies to retain some legacy processes and data while adopting new ones.

When companies want to modernize certain parts of their processes but also preserve critical customizations and data from the old system.

Features:

  • It allows for selective data migration, enabling you to bring over relevant historical data and customizations without carrying over everything.
  • Offers the flexibility to redesign and optimize parts of the system while retaining valuable aspects from the legacy system.
  • Reduces downtime compared to Greenfield and allows better customization compared to Brownfield.
  • Often involves the use of specialized tools to filter out unwanted data and processes, enabling selective transformation.

Use Case: Ideal for organizations that want to take advantage of S/4HANA's new features while selectively transferring necessary custom developments, processes, and data.

Other Approaches

Selective Data Transition: This is sometimes considered a variant of bluefield, where specific data and configurations are migrated rather than doing a full system conversion or a full reimplementation.

Phased or Iterative Migrations: In these cases, parts of the system or specific modules are converted incrementally to S/4HANA, rather than performing a full conversion all at once.

Summary of differences:

These three approaches (greenfield, brownfield, bluefield) cover the main strategies for S/4HANA migrations, and each organization chooses based on its specific needs, resources, and goals.

So what to choose? My experience and industry recommendation is Bluefield, which offers more flexibility and the ability to modernize selectively, whereas Brownfield is faster but less transformative.

Q) SAP is speaking a lot about Clean Core. Which one is more relevant in this context?

As per my knowledge, the Bluefield approach is more aligned with the Clean Core concept in SAP S/4HANA.

Here’s why:

  • Clean Core Concept: The idea of a clean core is to minimize or completely avoid customizations in the core SAP system, ensuring that the core remains as standard as possible. Any required customizations should be done using side-by-side extensions or through the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), rather than modifying the core directly. This enables easier future upgrades, better system stability, and enhanced use of cloud innovations.
  • Bluefield’s Relevance:
  • In a Bluefield migration, you have the ability to selectively migrate custom developments, business processes, and data. This allows you to critically evaluate which customizations are truly necessary and eliminate unnecessary custom code that would otherwise clutter the system.
  • It supports modernization of your landscape by allowing you to adopt new, standard SAP best practices for certain areas, while keeping only the required custom elements in external environments or using modern extensibility frameworks.You can move toward a clean core by offloading custom code into more flexible extension options, ensuring the core SAP S/4HANA system remains standard and optimized for upgrades.

On the other hand, Brownfield typically carries over all existing configurations and customizations, which is counterproductive to maintaining a clean core, as it could perpetuate legacy customizations and technical debt.

Thus, Bluefield is the better choice if your goal is to implement a Clean Core strategy.

Q) What additional points I must consider with regards to Security & Authorization in SAP?

When considering Security & Authorization in an SAP S/4HANA migration or conversion project, whether it's Bluefield or Brownfield, there are critical factors you must address to ensure the system remains secure and compliant post-migration.

1. Role Design and Optimization:

  • Review Existing Roles: In a Brownfield migration, all legacy roles and authorizations are typically carried over. This can result in outdated or redundant roles that do not align with S/4HANA's simplified processes. For a Clean Core strategy, assess and clean up roles to ensure they match new processes and business needs.
    • Simplified Data Model: S/4HANA has a simplified data model, which may affect authorization objects linked to tables, transactions, and business processes. You will need to map legacy roles to the new S/4HANA authorization objects.
    • Optimize for Fiori: S/4HANA heavily uses SAP Fiori as the front-end, which comes with a role-based access control (RBAC) model. Ensure that roles are optimized for Fiori apps, as Fiori apps typically involve multiple underlying backend authorizations.

2. Clean Core and Extensibility:

  • Avoid Custom Code in the Core: With the Clean Core concept, custom developments should be moved to external platforms (e.g., SAP Business Technology Platform). Ensure that custom authorization objects related to those developments are also managed outside of the core, where applicable.
  • Extension Model: If your migration uses SAP’s new side-by-side extensibility framework or enhancements via BTP, you must ensure that proper security mechanisms are in place for these external apps, including secure communication, OAuth, and SAML for authentication.

3. Segregation of Duties (SoD) and GRC Alignment:

  • Review SoD Conflicts: S/4HANA introduces new functionalities and removes obsolete ones, so it's essential to review and update your SoD rulesets to account for these changes. Ensure that your SAP GRC Access Control is aligned with the new S/4HANA environment.
  • GRC Integration: Make sure that GRC Access Control, Process Control, and Risk Management are integrated into the new environment to continuously monitor authorizations, SoD, and other risk factors.

NOTE: With the new SAP GRC embedded model, all the GRC components such as AC, PC, and RM will become embedded on S4 HANA. You may also consider evaluating it, if your S4 HANA migration doesn’t have it in the list.

4. New Security Features in S/4HANA:

  • S/4HANA Security Innovations: Leverage S/4HANA’s enhanced security features like Context-Based Authorizations (CBA), Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), and UI Masking or UI Logging to protect sensitive data.
  • Cloud and Hybrid Security: If you are moving to S/4HANA Cloud or adopting a hybrid model, ensure you follow SAP's best practices for cloud security (including SAP Cloud Identity Services) and managing secure access to both on-premise and cloud systems.

5. Compliance and Data Privacy:

  • Data Protection Compliance: Ensure that the authorization design complies with GDPR, CCPA, or any other applicable data privacy regulations. This includes defining clear roles for access to personal or sensitive data and implementing data masking or encryption mechanisms where necessary.
  • Audit and Logging: S/4HANA offers enhanced capabilities for audit logging and monitoring. Ensure that proper logging of sensitive transactions and authorization changes is implemented to facilitate compliance audits.

6. SAML, OAuth, and Single Sign-On (SSO):

  • Enable SSO: Ensure that Single Sign-On (SSO) solutions, such as SAML 3.0 or OAuth 2.0, are correctly set up for secure and seamless access across different systems. This is particularly important in environments that use both cloud and on-premise systems.
  • SAP Identity Authentication and Provisioning: Consider using SAP Cloud Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning Services for secure and centralized user management across your SAP and non-SAP applications.

7. User Experience and Authorization Consistency:

  • Consistent User Experience: Since S/4HANA relies heavily on Fiori applications, ensure that users have consistent authorization experiences across different devices (mobile, desktop) and application layers (back-end, Fiori, etc.).
  • Catalog and Group Management: In Fiori, roles are managed using catalogs and groups (spaces and pages). You should make sure the Fiori launchpad roles are properly configured to give users access only to the apps they need.

8. Testing and Validation:

  • Security Testing: Post-migration, conduct rigorous security testing, including penetration testing, vulnerability assessments, and role-based access reviews, to ensure that all aspects of the new system, including extensions and external apps, are secure.
  • Authorization Testing: Run authorization testing to ensure that users have only the access they need and that there are no excessive privileges carried over from legacy systems.

By addressing these security and authorization concerns, you can ensure that your SAP S/4HANA migration is secure, compliant, and optimized for modern business processes.

Q) What is context based authorization?

Context-Based Authorization (CBA) in SAP refers to a dynamic approach to controlling access based on the context in which a user is attempting to perform an action. Unlike traditional role-based authorization, which grants or restricts access solely based on predefined roles and permissions, CBA takes into account additional contextual factors at the time of the authorization check.

Key Elements of Context-Based Authorization:

1. Dynamic Contextual Factors:

  • CBA goes beyond static roles and evaluates additional parameters like:
    Location: Where the user is logging in from (e.g., specific countries or regions).
    Time: The time of day or the business period during which the user is trying to access the system (e.g., access during business hours vs. after-hours).
    Device: The type of device the user is using (e.g., laptop, mobile, or tablet).
    User Attributes: Attributes tied to the user profile, such as department, job function, or security clearance level.
    Data Sensitivity: Sensitivity level of the data being accessed (e.g., financial vs. non-sensitive information).

This means that even if a user has a role that grants them access, the system can deny or modify their access depending on the context.

2. Granular Control:

  • CBA allows for fine-grained access control, meaning that specific conditions can be set for individual transactions or actions.
  • For example, a financial manager may be allowed to approve expenses up to a certain amount but only when connected to the corporate network and during business hours. If they try to approve the expense remotely or after-hours, the authorization may be restricted.

3. Improved Security:

  • Adaptive Security: By factoring in real-time contextual information, CBA improves security by adapting to potential risks. For example, the system might detect that an access request is coming from an unfamiliar location or outside normal business hours and take precautionary measures such as denying access or triggering additional authentication.
  • Risk Mitigation: It helps mitigate risks by ensuring that sensitive transactions or data can only be accessed under secure, controlled conditions.

4. Flexibility and Compliance:

  • Regulatory Compliance: Many industries are subject to strict compliance regulations that may require dynamic access controls, especially concerning sensitive data like Personally Identifiable Information (PII). CBA helps meet these requirements by ensuring that access to data is aligned with specific contextual conditions.
  • Business Flexibility: Organizations can adapt access controls to meet business requirements without hardcoding rules in the system, providing flexibility to handle exceptional situations without compromising security.

Example Use Cases:

  1. Field Service Management: A field engineer may be allowed to access customer maintenance data while they are on-site at a customer’s location, but not when they are outside the location or during non-working hours.
  2. Finance and HR: A payroll manager might have access to salary data but only within the secure office network. If the manager tries to access the same information from a public Wi-Fi network, access could be blocked or restricted to read-only.
  3. Sensitive Transactions: A sales manager can create purchase orders for high-value items but only from approved devices. If they use a mobile device, they may only be able to view sales reports but not execute high-value transactions.

Implementation in SAP:

  • Dynamic Authorization Management: CBA is often implemented using a combination of SAP Authorization Objects, ABAP coding for dynamic checks, and Security Policies that define context-based rules.
  • SAP GRC: In some cases, CBA can be integrated with SAP Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) tools to enforce risk-based authorizations, ensuring that the risk profile of the transaction and context is evaluated before access is granted.
  • Integration with Fiori and BTP: For systems using SAP Fiori or the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), context-based rules can be implemented to dynamically grant or restrict access to specific Fiori apps based on contextual information.

Benefits:

  • Enhanced Security: Limits access based on real-time conditions, reducing the attack surface.
  • Reduced Risk of Misuse: Controls over sensitive transactions and data access prevent unauthorized activities even from users with high-level roles.
  • Increased Flexibility: Business processes remain fluid without hardcoded access restrictions, as the system adjusts access based on the situation.
  • Better Compliance: Ensures that sensitive information is accessed in a controlled, compliant manner.

Context-Based Authorization in SAP provides dynamic and adaptive access control based on real-time contextual factors, enhancing security while maintaining flexibility and compliance in the organization’s access management strategy.

Wrapping up:

In conclusion, when it comes to your S/4HANA transformation journey, the strategy you choose depends on your organization’s unique needs and future landscape. Each approach offers its own set of advantages and drawbacks, so it’s not a simple, one-size-fits-all comparison.

The key is to select a strategy that aligns best with your goals. However, one critical factor to consider is maintaining a "Clean Core" This principle is likely to become a major focus in future transformations, and adhering to it now can help you avoid unnecessary rework down the road.

In short, while there are many ways to approach your S/4HANA transformation, prioritizing a Clean Core can streamline the process and future-proof your system.

entry.title: SIOS LifeKeeper for High Availability of SAP ASCS /ERS HA and HANA Database on Azure
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP HANA is a high-performance in-memory database that supports real-time analytics and transactions. It is widely used by enterprises to run their critical business applications and processes. However, SAP HANA systems also require high availability and disaster recovery solutions to ensure business continuity and data protection in case of failures or disasters.

Azure is a cloud platform that offers a range of services and features to host, manage, and scale SAP HANA systems. Azure provides high-performance virtual machines, storage, networking, and security options that can meet the requirements of SAP HANA workloads. However, Azure does not offer a native high availability solution for SAP HANA systems, which means that customers need to use third-party tools or custom scripts to achieve high availability for their SAP HANA databases on Azure.

SIOS LifeKeeper is a proven high availability and disaster recovery solution that can protect SAP HANA systems on Azure. SIOS LifeKeeper can monitor, detect, and recover from failures of SAP HANA instances, virtual machines, storage, network, or Azure services. SIOS LifeKeeper can also replicate data between primary and secondary SAP HANA systems, and perform automatic or manual failover and failback operations. SIOS LifeKeeper can integrate with Azure services such as Azure Load Balancer, Azure Traffic Manager, and Azure Site Recovery to provide a comprehensive and reliable high availability solution for SAP HANA systems on Azure.

Prerequisites

Before you can configure and deploy a highly available SAP HANA system on Azure using SIOS LifeKeeper, you need to have the following prerequisites:

  • An Azure subscription and an Azure account with sufficient permissions to create and manage resources.
  • A SAP HANA license and installation media.
  • A SIOS LifeKeeper license and installation media.
  • Two or more Azure virtual machines that meet the SAP HANA hardware and software requirements. You can use the Azure Marketplace to create SAP HANA certified virtual machines.
  • A shared storage solution that supports SAP HANA data and log volumes. You can use Azure NetApp Files, Azure Files, or Azure Shared Disks as shared storage options.
  • A network solution that allows communication between the SAP HANA systems and the SIOS LifeKeeper nodes. You can use Azure Virtual Network, Azure VPN Gateway, or Azure ExpressRoute as network options.
  • A load balancer solution that can distribute traffic to the SAP HANA systems and the SIOS LifeKeeper nodes. You can use Azure Load Balancer, Azure Application Gateway, or Azure Traffic Manager as load balancer options.

Configuration Steps

After you have prepared the prerequisites, you can follow these steps to configure and deploy a highly available SAP HANA system on Azure using SIOS LifeKeeper:

  1. Install SAP HANA on the primary virtual machine and configure the SAP HANA data and log volumes on the shared storage.
  2. Install SAP ASCS  on the primary virtual machine and SAP ERS on Secondary Virtual Machine and /sapmnt on the shared storage.
  3. Install SIOS LifeKeeper on the primary virtual machine and join it to a SIOS LifeKeeper cluster.
  4. Create a SIOS LifeKeeper SAP HANA resource hierarchy on the primary virtual machine and configure the SAP HANA instance details and the shared storage paths.
  5. Install SAP HANA on the secondary virtual machine and configure the SAP HANA data and log volumes on the shared storage.
  6. Install SIOS LifeKeeper on the secondary virtual machine and join it to the same SIOS LifeKeeper cluster as the primary virtual machine.
  7. Extend the SIOS LifeKeeper SAP HANA resource hierarchy from the primary virtual machine to the secondary virtual machine and configure the replication mode and the failover policies.
  8. Configure the load balancer to direct traffic to the SAP HANA systems and the SIOS LifeKeeper nodes.
  9. Test the high availability and disaster recovery scenarios by simulating failures and verifying the failover and failback operations.

entry.title: SAP JAVA - How Memory allocation works here?, How Garbage Collection helps?, print memhist screen
entry.content:
Context: A job failed in the system --> and I can see some Allocation Failure logs at that time, check below screenshot what is there in the log

J 5106135.671: [GC (Allocation Failure) 5106135.671: [ParNewJ

: 6473015K->163897K(7340032K), 0.3083848 secs] 17449842K->11141057K(24117248K), 0.3087756 secs] [Times: user=0.21 sys=0.00 real=0.31 secs]

 5107049.163: [GC (Allocation Failure) 5107049.164: [ParNewJ

: 5962034K->71890K(7340032K), 5.4469845 secs] 16939193K->13683627K(24117248K), 5.4474075 secs] [Times: user=4.22 sys=0.01 real=5.45 secs]
J 5107054.650: [GC CMS Initial Mark[1 CMS-initial-mark: 13611736K(16777216K)] 15000842K(24117248K), 0.0449463 secs] [Times: user=0.08 sys=0.00 real=0.05 secs]

J 5107054.695: [CMS-concurrent-mark-start]

J 5107055.731: [CMS-concurrent-mark: 0.840/1.036 secs] [Times: user=0.38 sys=0.00 real=1.03 secs]
J 5107055.731: [CMS-concurrent-preclean-start]
J 5107055.769: [CMS-concurrent-preclean: 0.038/0.039 secs] [Times: user=0.01 sys=0.00 real=0.04 secs]
J 5107055.771: [CMS-concurrent-abortable-preclean-start]

J CMS: abort preclean due to time 5107060.902: [CMS-concurrent-abortable-preclean: 4.609/5.132 secs] [Times: user=1.49 sys=0.01 real=5.13 secs]

J 5107060.903: [GC CMS Final Remark[YG occupancy: 2792157 K (7340032 K)]5107060.904: [Rescan (parallel) , 0.0458228 secs]5107060.949: [weak refs processing, 0.0259838 secs]5107060.980: [class unloading
, 0.3621919 secs]5107061.342: [scrub symbol tableJ

, 0.1064461 secs]5107061.449: [scrub string table, 0.0072483 secs][1 CMS-remark: 13611736K(16777216K)] 16403894K(24117248K), 0.5528646 secs] [Times: user=0.20 sys=0.01 real=0.56 secs]

J 5107062.078: [CMS-concurrent-sweep: 0.586/0.621 secs] [Times: user=0.30 sys=0.00 real=0.62 secs]
J 5107062.078: [CMS-concurrent-reset-start]
J 5107062.085: [CMS-concurrent-reset: 0.007/0.008 secs] [Times: user=0.00 sys=0.00 real=0.01 secs]

Let's Understand what is happening in this above log.

This issue is related to the Garbage Collection (GC) process, specifically due to CMS (Concurrent Mark-Sweep) Garbage Collection and allocation failures. This often happens when the JVM is unable to allocate enough memory in the Young Generation (ParNew) or Old Generation (CMS) because the memory has become fragmented or too full to handle the request efficiently.

  1. GC (Allocation Failure) in ParNew:

    • In your logs, the GC (Allocation Failure) happens several times:
      • ParNew is triggered (Young Generation GC), which occurs when the JVM cannot allocate memory for new objects in the Young Generation.
         
         
        [GC (Allocation Failure) 5106135.671: [ParNewJ 6473015K->163897K(7340032K), 0.3083848 secs] 17449842K->11141057K(24117248K), 0.3087756 secs]
         
      • This log shows that the Young Generation was full, leading to a GC event. After GC, 6473015K (roughly 6.17 GB) of data was reduced to 163897K (~160 MB), which means most of the objects were cleared out, but this took time.
      • The overall heap usage was reduced from 17.4 GB to 11.1 GB, leaving about 6 GB in the Old Generation.

  2. CMS Garbage Collection in the Old Generation:

    • The CMS (Concurrent Mark-Sweep) GC is also triggered for the Old Generation:
       
      [GC CMS Initial Mark[1 CMS-initial-mark: 13611736K(16777216K)] 15000842K(24117248K), 0.0449463 secs]
       
      • Here, we see the CMS GC marking phase starts, and it reports 13.6 GB of the Old Generation (out of 16 GB) is in use.
      • CMS is a non-compacting collector, which means it doesn't reorganize memory, leading to memory fragmentation. Over time, even though there may be free memory available in the Old Generation, fragmentation can prevent the JVM from allocating large contiguous memory blocks.

  3. Long GC Pauses and Concurrent GC Phases:

    • We observe long GC times:
      • One GC event took 5.44 seconds:
         
        5962034K->71890K(7340032K), 5.4469845 secs] 16939193K->13683627K(24117248K), 5.4474075 secs
         
      • This long pause indicates that garbage collection is struggling to free memory, which could be due to a large number of objects being tenured (moved to Old Generation) or due to fragmentation.

Why is the Job Failing?

Even though the total heap space seems adequate, the following issues are contributing to the failure:

  1. Fragmentation in the Old Generation (CMS Collector):

    • CMS is prone to fragmentation because it doesn’t compact memory. If there is fragmentation in the Old Generation, the JVM may not be able to find a contiguous block of memory large enough to allocate new objects, even though the total available memory seems sufficient.
    • When CMS cannot allocate memory in the Old Generation due to fragmentation, the JVM will trigger GC (Allocation Failure) events, and eventually, if the job needs a large contiguous block of memory, it will fail.

  2. High Old Generation Usage:

    • In the logs, we see the Old Generation usage grows to about 13.6 GB (out of 16 GB). While there’s technically 2.4 GB of free space, fragmentation might prevent the JVM from allocating that space efficiently.

  3. Young Generation Full:

    • The Young Generation (ParNew) is constantly reaching its capacity, triggering GC. This indicates the application is creating many short-lived objects that the JVM struggles to process in a timely manner.

  4. Full GC and Long Pauses:

    • The CMS GC is leading to long GC pause times (5.44 seconds, 5.13 seconds). These long GC pauses indicate that the JVM is spending significant time in garbage collection and is struggling to free up space.

  5. Concurrent Mark-Sweep Phases:

    • CMS phases like Initial Mark, Concurrent Mark, Preclean, Sweep are happening continuously, which suggests high memory churn. If these phases are interrupted or fail to clear enough space, the JVM won’t have enough available memory to allocate new objects for your job.

Then to get more details of memory issue, you can use jvmmon tool -- I have used

command: print memhist, and below is the screen from that, let's check what is in this screen?

Breakdown of the JVM Metrics:

1. heap-comm and heap-used:

  • heap-comm (Committed Heap): This is the total amount of memory reserved (or committed) for the Java heap. In this case, it shows 23.0g (23 GB), meaning the JVM has reserved 23 GB of memory for the heap.
  • heap-used (Used Heap): This represents the amount of memory actually being used in the heap. It is shown as 10.9g (10.9 GB), meaning 10.9 GB of the 23 GB heap is currently in use.

2. meta-comm and meta-used:

  • meta-comm (Committed MetaSpace): The MetaSpace is an area of memory used by the JVM to store class metadata (class definitions, methods, etc.). This value represents how much MetaSpace has been reserved. It shows 938m (938 MB).
  • meta-used (Used MetaSpace): This is the amount of memory actually being used in MetaSpace. It's listed as 674m (674 MB), meaning that 674 MB of the 938 MB committed space is being used.

3. meta-csc and meta-csu:

  • meta-csc (Class Space Committed): This represents the memory committed for storing class metadata specifically for the classes loaded by the JVM. In this case, it's 117m (117 MB).
  • meta-csu (Class Space Used): This is the amount of that space actually being used by loaded classes. The value is 71m (71 MB), meaning that 71 MB out of the 117 MB committed class space is currently in use.

4. meta-gctr (GC Threshold):

  • meta-gctr: This represents the GC threshold for the MetaSpace. It's the point at which a garbage collection (GC) will be triggered to reclaim unused class metadata. In your case, the value is 1.6g (1.6 GB). This means that when MetaSpace usage approaches this value, the JVM may trigger GC to free up unused class metadata.

5. code (Code Cache):

  • code (Code Cache Committed): The Code Cache is a special region where the JVM stores compiled native code generated by the Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler. This value represents how much space is reserved for the code cache. In this case, it's 230m (230 MB).

6. nmt-mlc, nmt-map, nmt-gc, nmt-oth, nmt-ovh:

  • These values relate to Native Memory Tracking (NMT), a diagnostic feature of the JVM used to track memory allocation outside the Java heap.
    • nmt-mlc: Memory allocated via the malloc function by the HotSpot JVM.
    • nmt-map: Memory allocated by memory-mapped files (mmap).
    • nmt-gc: Memory allocated due to GC overhead (malloc and mmap) by the JVM.
    • nmt-oth: Memory allocations that are categorized as "other" (such as direct byte buffers or unsafe memory allocations).
    • nmt-ovh: NMT tracking overhead.
  • These fields are only populated when NMT is active, but they are not shown in this log excerpt.

7. jthr-num and jthr-nd:

  • jthr-num (Number of Java Threads): The total number of active threads in the JVM, including both daemon and non-daemon threads. It shows 391 threads in this case.
  • jthr-nd (Non-Daemon Threads): The number of non-daemon threads (i.e., threads that prevent the JVM from shutting down when they are running). In your data, this is 341.

8. jthr-cr (Threads Created):

  • jthr-cr (Threads Created [delta]): The number of threads created since the last measurement (delta means the change in value between two data points). In this snapshot, it is 0, meaning no new threads were created since the last check.

9. jthr-st (Thread Stack Reserved):

  • jthr-st: This refers to the total reserved size of Java thread stacks, which is not shown here as the [nmt] section is inactive.

10. cldg-num and cldg-anon:

  • cldg-num (Classloader Data Number): The number of ClassLoader Data instances in the JVM. This value indicates how many class loaders are actively managing classes. It's not displayed in this log snippet.
  • cldg-anon (Anonymous ClassLoader Data): The number of anonymous class loader data instances. These are typically used for dynamically generated classes.

11. cls-num, cls-ld, cls-uld:

  • cls-num (Classes Loaded): The total number of classes currently loaded by the JVM, including instance and array classes. In this log, the value is 103,686.
  • cls-ld (Classes Loaded [delta]): The number of new classes loaded since the last measurement. In this case, it is 0.
  • cls-uld (Classes Unloaded [delta]): The number of classes unloaded since the last measurement. It is also 0, indicating no classes were unloaded.

 

#sap #sapcommunity #sapnw7.5 #linux #sapjvm #jvmmon #sapjavamemoryissue

entry.title: 🧰👷‍♂️ The SAP S/4HANA RISE & SAP BTP - Toolbox 👷‍♂️🧰
entry.content:
The SAP S/4HANA PCE Private Cloud Edition on RISE & SAP BTP

    🧰👷‍Toolbox for SAP Technology Architects👷‍🧰

 

 

Having found this "RISE & BTP 1 Pager for Technical Architects", if you're interested in this subject, then subscribe to the SAP Innovation and Transformation newsletter, here's the link:  Innovation & Transformation Newsletter SAP Services and Support

RISE with SAP (S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition on SAP RISE) is re-writing the book on how companies "do" SAP, and infact, everybody on that journey or planning for or preparing for that journey is re-writing the book.

There are so many myths and misunderstandings about RISE, hopefully this work will shine more light on the subject.

The goal of this work is to provide a SAP RISE "1 Pager" for all of us Customer/System Integrator side folks who are on this journey. 

And back in 2013 when I was studying for SAP HANA Certification I published the following blog, as a central point of reference for SAP HANA technical information, it helped me with what I was studying and helped others with having piles of very useful information in one place:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/the-sap-hana-reference-for-sap-basis-administrators/ba-p/13007539

Fast forward 12 years, and we are now in the same situation with SAP S/4HANA PCE on RISE and the BTP, there is useful documentation all over the place and it would help if there was a central location for SAP Technology Architects to use as a "Toolbox". It will certainly help me 🙂

Implementing and Migrating to and Running S/4 PCE on RISE for the last three years, and as usual collecting a mountain of the most useful documentation as a personal toolbox, it's been clear to see that similar to the situation back in the earlier period of HANA, there is useful RISE documentation in a number of locations, this blog will act as a central resource pointing to all of the locations of the SAP S/4 PCE RISE documentation.

And similarly for the BTP, having been doing BTP (or as it was the SAP Cloud Platform) for 7 years, this blog will be a central location pointing to the most useful BTP documentation for SAP Technology Architects.

The two subjects are combined, SAP S/4HANA PCE on RISE with BTP, because in my eyes the two go hand in hand, S/4HANA PCE on RISE is the next generation of the previous On-Premise ERP offerings from SAP, and the BTP contains the next generation of Applications which we will all use with the S/4HANA PCE on RISE, including for example, and not limited to, Integration Suite and CPI & EM/AEM & APIM, obviously CALM as this is replacing in many but not all cases what we do with SolMan, and all of the other Services which we will need pretty much day by day to run S/4HANA PCE on RISE, and of course, Steampunk, keeping the Core Clean, and "Upgrade Stable" (as my friend Sakthivel Sivaguru would say), then we need the ABAP Service in the BTP for the side car extensions. But that's just the BTP "Starter Pack", as you go on the BTP journey you will be using all kinds of Services and including the AI Joule and the Business Application Studio and the Work Zone, Custom Domain, ADS, Secure Logon, CTMS  etc and then of course the Intelligent Services across the Business Processes, eg Intelligent Account Reconciliation, there's a link to an Excel sheet here, which lists all of the Business Processes and how they are Intelligently enabled by SAP Technologies.

If you have links to useful documentation which you think will help others and should be added to the list, then please just write it in the comments and I will add it to the list.

For 25 years I have been hunting and collecting SAP documentation, my library spans the whole journey from before the New Dimension Products, through NetWeaver 2004, through HANA DB, through S/4HANA, through SAP Cloud Platform and on through the current phase S/4HANA Cloud aka "Grow with SAP", S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition aka "RISE with SAP", and the Business Technology Platform and the Intelligent Technologies. Hunting a collecting documentation is a useful skill, and here's a tip from my friend Jarod Sandham - some useful search criteria to find documentation is to search "https://assets.dm.ux.sap.com/ <insert search criteria here>, eg open google and search for:  https://assets.dm.ux.sap.com/  security

Please expect to find changes and new information here, because it will be a live work in progress, it's going to take a few weeks to list and classify everything that I already have. Plans for immediate future are for each link to add a screenshot of the most useful information behind that link, and that's going to take a few weeks, and at the same time the list will just get longer.

And finally the ask to SAP... if friends from SAP are reading this work, and know about new versions of documents/materials or new documents/materials which are available either publicly or with an S User and could enrich this work, please let me know either directly or in the chat and I will incorporate that material to the benefit of everyone.

Disclaimer: For the record, regarding the pictures and screenshots, ALL pictures are from documents which are publicly available and searchable on google, there are no pictures from any SAP documentation which is protected behind an authentication mechanism. There are many links here to documents where you will need to have an S User to be able to read them, and there are no screenshots from any of those documents. Every screenshot/picture contains a statement that this document is publicly available on the Internet.

This is the journey you are going on...

 

Source: 20230328_sap_dmlt.pdf

 

Source:  20230328_sap_dmlt.pdf

 

 

Ok, let's go 🚀

 

 

Contents:

Section 1] General Introduction Documents and RISE Getting Started Guides

Section 1.01] The SAP & Customer Shared Responsibilities on RISE

Section 1.02] The S/4HANA 3 Transition & Migration & Implementation Options

Section 1.1] Implementing and Running S/4HANA PCE Private Cloud Edition on SAP RISE

Section 2] SAP ECS Enterprise Cloud Services

Section 2.1] Azure Useful Links

Section 2.2] AWS Amazon Web Services Useful Links

Section 3] Security on SAP RISE and ECS 

Section 4] Data Migration and Transition

Section 5] SAP BTP General

Section 5.1] SAP CALM Cloud Application LifeCycle Management

Section 5.1.1] Monitoring with SAP CALM - UNDER PREPARATION

Section 5.1.2] SAP Build Work Zone

Section 5.2] Extending S/4HANA on RISE - UNDER PREPARATION

Section 5.3] Integrating S/4HANA on RISE - UNDER PREPARATION

Section 6] SAP Customer Center of Excellence

Section 7] Lessons Learned

_________________________________________________________________

 

Section 1] General Introduction Documents and RISE Getting Started Guides

What is RISE from the viewpoint of a SAP Technology Architect ?  

The following picture is from the publicly available document Presentation Title

 

This one below is from here:  PowerPoint Presentation

 

 

 

 

How do you do RISE ?

Regardless of what anybody thinks about all of the different methodologies, every SAP Customer who is contemplating the move to S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition on RISE must begin by reading about the "RISE with SAP Methodology".

This is basically a guided procedure including supporting documentation to help SAP Customers get to S/4HANA in the smoothest and straightest line possible. 

Naturally SAP have a recommended tool chain to support for following the SAP RISE Methodology, and regardless of whether on not you use SAP's recommended tools, this picture is very useful because it elaborates a little more what is happening at each part of the journey:

Here's an introduction to the methodology RISE with SAP | Methodology

This is the overview of the guided procedure:

This picture comes from this document which is publicly available: Mapping your journey to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition : A practical guide for senior IT leadership

These are the core tasks of the methodology:

 

day_1_session_2b_pnp_application_modernization_migration.pdf

I recommend everybody who will be part of the journey to read that document as one of the first steps, once everybody has read that document, everybody will be on the same page as to what lies ahead.

Section 1.01] The SAP & Customer Shared Responsibilities on RISE

This section is exceptionally important, from the SAP Basis and Operations perspective there are naturally a lot of questions around, how does the Basis function work on RISE. What does SAP do, what does the Customer (and their SI) do ? There are a lot of misunderstandings and incorrect myths.

The goal of this section is to help the SAP Customer who is thinking about RISE to build a cleaer picture and understanding of what the responsibilities are and what the segregation of duties are.

A lot of people seem to think that on RISE the Customer won't need a Basis Team, well, far from it, on RISE the Customer obviously won't need so many Basis people, but, and the big but is, the Customer will need exceptionally qualified and experienced Basis people who at the same time have a very good maturity and discipline in their behaviour. One of my biggest lessons, and there are many many learnings, has been, with RISE, due to the nature of the shared responsibilities, there is very little room for trial and error in the Basis Domain, and the Basis Team need to put massive effort in to proper planning and preparation of activities and understand who will do what bases upon the segregation of duties between SAP and the Customer. There is no room for trial and error when doing new things in the Basis area.

So, we could write a book about that, but we are here to share useful documentation, so here it comes...

RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition Security and Compliance

Don't be deceived by the title, this document contains some golden information, anybody wanting to understand who does what with RISE, here are pictures you need, these picture show where your responsibilities are:

We start with a higher level view which is from the RISE OnBoarding Handbook Onboarding Handbook .pdf

In my opinion the above picture is not detailed enough, it doesn't communicated really how much responsibility the Customer SAP Basis Team will have. (The detailed list of ECS Services is here: SAP S/4HANA Cloud private edition - Service Specifications)

The following pictures go into some more detail on the Customer's SAP Basis responsibilities:

The above pictures are from the publicly available document Check & ProductivityPlus Training

RISE with SAP: Shared Security Responsibility for SAP Cloud Services

There are some great pictures and explanations in this blog, like this one:

 

Speaking from first hand experience this is how I see the responsibilities, this can of course all by done by SAP ECS by the Customer buying additional Services:

Customer / SI

Dedicated Private Connectivity to Hyperscaler
Configuration of Business Processes
Application Administration and Management
Application Identity Management
Application Authentication and Authorisation
Business Roles, User Groups, Access Control
Compliance Regulations
Application Security Audit Logs
API Extension, Integration and 3rd Party Connectivity
Application Change Management
Performance Management
Advanced Security & Compliance
Functional Application Management
Deployment Management
Testing
Release Management
Business Improvement
Data LifeCycle Management
Operations Improvement
Advanced Application / Solution Monitoring
Application Security Updates
Continuous Operations

SAP ECS

SW Subscription / Support
Reference Architecture
Technical System Operations
HA / DR
OS/ DB
Compute/Storage/Network
Threat Management and Patch Management
Operational Security

Section 1.02] The S/4HANA 3 Transition & Migration & Implementation Options

There are three ways to get to S/4HANA PCE on RISE:

Greenfield - new clean S/4HANA

Bluefield Selective Data Transition - search this page for Bluefield and you will find the latest guides on how to do this

Brownfield Transformation - transform and convert an existing ECC in to an S/4HANA

This document is publicly available: 20230328_sap_dmlt.pdf

I'm not going to write much more on this in my own words because there is so much very useful documentation available from SAP on this subject.

This section of the Toolbox will provide links to lots of useful supporting documents and highlight very useful diagrams which will help for understanding your situation and your options.

It's less about tossing a coin and more about understanding what you want to achieve and where you want to get to. The following documents and pictures will explain why...

Please note some of these documents are older and some are newer, the older ones are using older terminology, but the essentials are the same and that's why they are included here, because the message they communicate is useful

These pictures are from this publicly available document: Microsoft PowerPoint - S4HANA RIG @ User Groups.pptx

 

 

 

The following picture is from this publicly available document: PowerPoint Presentation

 

 

The following three pictures are from a really good publicly available RIG document from March 2024: GSSP workshop Day 1 - Part 1

 

 

 

Here is another publicly available excellent picture:  day_1_session_2b_pnp_application_modernization_migration.pdf

Match your business objectives to possible transition paths...

 

 

Do you want to do Process ReUse or Process ReDesign, from the same presentation as the one above..

 

This is a really useful document, it walks you through a theoretical example demo S/4HANA Transformation and including all of the steps Transformation Plan 1.0 - Outcome summary of SAP S/4HANA Adoption Starter

and some very useful pictures....

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Section 1.1] Implementing and Running S/4HANA PCE Private Cloud Edition on SAP RISE

There are three ways to get to S/4HANA PCE on RISE:

Greenfield - new clean S/4HANA

Bluefield - search this page for Bluefield and you will find the latest guides on how to do this

Brownfield - transform an existing ECC in to an S/4HANA

There is a very nice document here FRE Best Practice Guide for RISE Transformation_Private Cloud.pdf which amongst other things has some nice picture and details on SAP's recommended architectures for the different S/4HANA migration/transformation approaches including:

 

The following two pictures are Brownfield, meaning you Transform your existing ERP in to an S/4HANA on RISE:

 

 

These three pictures are publicly available in  this document: Mapping your journey to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition : A practical guide for senior IT leadership

The SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer

This is , the starting point, this is an unbelievable library of resources

You navigate it like this, click the link below, and then click Explore all Roadmaps

then click SAP Activate Methodology for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition on SAP RISE

then read the Overview and click any of the links and then click on Accelerators

Section 2] SAP ECS Enterprise Cloud Services

There is not too much information in the public domain on what the SAP ECS Team is doing, they are your Partner for pretty much everything, they are like your Cloud/Unix/Technical-Layer-Basis wrapped in to one, the majority of the really useful documentation on what the ECS Team do and how to work with them is listed below, but, you will need an S User and in some cases need to be a RISE Customer to access the documentation.

This section of the Toolbox is really aimed at Basis people and Unix Team / Cloud Hosting / Network people from the Customer side, and so, I am including here the most useful pictures from documents in the public domain to help with introductions as to how the networking to SAP ECS is working, how the hosting is working and how the security of it all is working.

The following picture is from the publicly available document Presentation Title

The picture helps us all to understand what the SAP ECS Team are doing, what parts of the stack they are responsible for:

This is a nice overview of SAP Cloud Application Services for RISE Customers, this document is publicly available: Presentation Title

 

 

And now some useful pictures on how the networking and HA and DR are working, these pictures are from the publicly available document  220818_rise_with_sap_sap_hana_cloud_private_edtition.pdf

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

And then here we have some great insights to the HA and DR setup... there are other documents, they are listed in this 1 Pager and you will need an S User for those, just search this list for HA and DR...

There is only one way to open the Security chapter of the 1 Pager and that is to begin with the....

For Security subjects on S/4HANA PCE on RISE you should start with this publicly available document: Check & ProductivityPlus Training

Section 5] SAP BTP General

SAP Business Technology Platform BTP is a massive Platform, and in my opinion will be bigger and more important than the S/4HANA for SAP Customers in the future. The BTP is a technology enabler containing all kinds of capabilities.

The first thing I am going to comment on, since this is a 1 Pager for SAP Technology Architects is the Technical Architecture of the BTP.

The BTP is extremely rich and flexible in how it can be set up and configured, and this can be a good thing and a bad thing, the biggest challenge is, making foundation layer mistakes which cost time and money to correct in the future.

For me to try show how complex the BTP is, we will first look at SAP's Future Ready Enterprise Framework, this is an approach to modularising maturity with the BTP and leading the Customer on a journey to BTP Architecture Excellence.

You've heard of a SAP Customer Center of Excellence, there is now a concept of SAP BTP Center of Excellence, this shows how complex and rich this subject is.

There is a beautiful publicly available presentation here  on the Future Ready Enterprise and the SAP BTP Future Ready Enterprise Unlock and Maximize the value of BTP

With wonderful slides like these...

 

 

 

You don't necessarily need to engage SAP's Services on this, at least by studying and digesting the picture you can think about how those dimensions apply to your Organisation and fill in the boxes in terms of the objectives and vision of your Organisation

 

 

 

These slides are a humbling reminder of how vast the BTP is, and how getting a decent maturity level in the BTP will help your Organisation achieve its goals

 

 

So now we know, the BTP is big, and it's complex, and it needs proper planning and architecting if we are going to get the most from it.

For  the first few years the SAP Cloud Platform was mainly SAP Cloud Platform Integration and a lot of people slipped in to a bad habit of associating the BTP with Integration capability, we it's a lot more than that and we all need to appreciate that.

The following document is another useful introduction to the SAP Future Ready Enterprise Framework:

Presentation Title

The following picture is here to show us just how complex the BTP is, and let us all reflect on where our Company's maturity level is with the BTP:

 

 

Just spend some time to study that table and think about where your Organisation's maturity level is and whether or not it would be a good investment to buy some BTP Training and Services from SAP. I am not trying to sell Services here, but I have learned the hard way that an investment in knowledge can save significant amounts in the future.

This picture which is from the same deck is to really show us what a journey BTP maturity is...

 

There is a very nice blog by @Pedro_Souza  on the same subject here The Future-Ready Enterprise: A Framework for SAP B... - SAP Community

The next step for the SAP Technology Architect is to read the BTP Admin Guide SAP BTP Administrator's Guide | SAP Help Portal  and read the section on SetUp and Plan Set Up and Plan | SAP Help Portal the whole section including the Governance Model. The BTP Admin Guide as a pdf..  SAP_Cloud_Platform.pdf

SAP BTP Platform Configuration Guide 

PlatformConfiguration.pdf

SAP BTP Common Concepts and Strategic Directio

Presentation Title

SAP BTP Data Management in a Nutshell

Title Goes Here and Here and Here

Then read the section Setting up your Account Model Setting Up Your Account Model | SAP Help Portal

The biggest lesson I can bring is, when architecting the BTP from a clean sheet of paper, one of the most important foundation steps is to correctly design the Stages / Directories / Sub-Accounts and Entitlements, the setting up of the Account Model, get this wrong and you will pay for it later.

BTP Domains / Stages / Directories / Sub-Accounts

Domain refers to the Capability, eg Cloud Integration, Work Zone, Build Process Automation

Stages refers to the type of environment, eg Dev, Test, Quality, UAT, Prod

Directory refers to the structuring elements for Sub-Accounts

Sub-Account let's you further structure according to your requirements

My recomendation is go for an Account Model with Directories and Sub-Accounts Account Model With Directories and Subaccounts | SAP Help Portal

The reason for this is:

You then have the possibility for all kinds of template BTP layouts according to the demands of your Organisation and at the same time you can leverage all of the benefits described above

 

 

 

And then as already described you profit from being able to leverage Authorisation and Entitlement features per Directory / BTP Domain

Setting Up Your Account Model | SAP Help Portal

To recap setting up the Account Model is probably the most important BTP Technical Architecture decision !

This is the Future Ready Enterprise document which is referred to in the RISE Onboarding Handbook FRE Best Practice Guide for RISE Transformation_Private Cloud.pdf

Next step go to the SAP Discovery Center and look at the BTP OnBoarding material SAP Discovery Center Mission Public Card - Live and OnDemand Webinars about SAP BTP Onboarding

 

And then go to the SAP BTP OnBoarding center SAP Business Technology Platform General Concepts

After doing all of this preparation, the next step is to Architect and Administer your BTP, and for that the next step is to read the SAP BTP Administrator's Guide SAP BTP Administrator's Guide | SAP Help Portal

 

For SAP Build and all of the Services around it have a look at this presentation

2023_sap_build_webinar_april_4.pdf

 

For the BTP another very good place to start is here: Future Ready Best Practice Guide for RISE Transformation_Private Cloud.pdf

The document contains very nice descriptions of the most important BTP Services and also Reference Architecture pictures recommending which BTP Services to have and where and why depending upon the type of move to S/4HANA on RISE that you are doing

 

 

 

and very nice BTP Services descriptions like this one for Security and Identity Management in the BTP

 

 

and then you can move on the following documents...

Section 6] SAP Customer Center of Excellence

(This section is a work in progress)

You might be asking yourself why in a SAP RISE Toolbox there are links to how to do a SAP CCOE ?

That's a great question, and the answer is that, most companies have been running/operating SAP the same way since 3.1h, and now with RISE the whole game is changing, the Roles and Responsibilities are changing and the Segregation of Duties is changing, between who does what, SAP, Customer, and if they have one the Customer's System Integrator. 

And that's why, during the S/4HANA RISE Transformation, you will be best off, if you take the opportunity and redesign how you run/operate SAP, the way you have run SAP for the ECC generation will not fit to how you run SAP in the RISE generation, and on top of this, isn't it time for "The Business" and "IT/SAP" to get closer together ?

SAP offer a fantastic service for helping Customers on this journey, S/4HANA & RISE is not only an ERP Transformation, it's also an Organisational Transformation, and you should look at the SAP North Start Architecture Services, in my 25 years of doing SAP these are the most valuable Services I have seen as North Star Architectures brings the knowledge of all of SAP's Customers and Best Practices on how to run SAP. 

https://www.sap.com/documents/2023/11/c272ac9a-9b7e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

Section 7] Lessons Learned

(this section is an ongoing work in progress)

Don't skip "Phase 0", plan to give Phase Zero the time it needs

Key Users

Key Users are one of the keys to a successful GoLive make sure they are trained and have all of the support they need

 
Key Users are your Ambassadors, make sure they have everything they need
 
Investment and Multi-Year Multiple Release/GoLive Transformations
 
Everything is relative, whatever you can afford, make sure to invest in the success of the first GoLive/Release,do not cut costs or corners on the first GoLive/Release, the first GoLive/Release MUST be a SUCCESS.
 
Subsequent GoLives/Releases will in general be a rinse and repeat of the template for how
the first GoLive/Release was executed and you can reduce costs on those GoLives/Releases
 
S/4 Data Transformation and Integration
 
We need to do Mapping and Transformations of Data, either at the SAP End or the other End - this is another challenge to the "Clean-Core" and the S/4 Transformations, there needs to be a principle, mappings/transformation must happen outside of the S/4
 
Technical GoLive should be separated / disconnected from the Business GoLive
 
Technical GoLive several months before Business GoLive

RISE Proactive Maintenance for S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

Contractual Maintenance Periods

SAP and Customer agree regular periods where there can be downtimes for SAP to do Maintenance

Plan this in your Release Planning

S/4HANA Upgrades

In your multi-year Release Plan, make room for S/4HANA Upgrades to enable you to benefit from the latest SAP innovations

Capacity

You need to care for your Capacity and Data Volume Management

Basis Projects and Operations

You need to re-design your Project Basis Team and Operations Basis Team and methods of working according to the segregation of duties with the RISE ECS Team

Run SGEN straight after systems are delivered and before GoLives

Performance Tuning & House Keeping  - that's your job

FUE Licensing Costs and SAP Roles and Authorisations

SAP RISE S/4HANA usage is measured and sized in what’s called FUE’s, Full Usage Equivalents​, pay attention to this

SAP RISE AntiVirus

VSI is a extra service, not mandatory as of today

EWA Report

Pay very close to attention to it, it will become one of your best friends

S/4HANA FailOver and DR

Understand what you really get with 99.7 and 99.9 regarding Availability Zones and Regions

S/4HANA Preparation

Before installation, specify all Security Requirements and get them included in the installation and post installation

RISE is about Segregation of Duties on the Basis Team

RISE is about running Basis as a Factory with clear Roles and Responsibilities and Methods or Working

RISE is about Proper Planning an Preparation

Basis Trial and Error way of working evolves to Planning and Preparation way of working

 
Service Requests
 
Quality
 
Right First Time
 
SAP do not quality control Service Requests
 
Learn the Templates
 
RISE Technical Architecture
 
Is not the same as OnPremise
 
A RISE LoadBalancer or Application Gateway, does not necessarily work the same way on RISE as it does in your own DataCenter/HyperScaler even if they are the same
 
The SAP RISE Architecture View
 
Get from SAP as much technical architecture views of the RISE "Customer Setup" as possible
 
SAP Technical Architects are the an integral part of the new Basis Team
 
Basis Team benefits from being experienced and disciplined 
 
RISE Architect from SAP
 
Get a SAP RISE Architect from SAP onboarded to the project from the beginning

SAP CALM is your new best friend

Sizing and Performance Testing better in than out

Trust and Verify
